coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots obtained from citric acid and L-phenylalanine
coredata.dc:creator: Kostromin, Sergei
Borodina, Anastasia
Pankin, Dmitrii
Povolotskiy, Alexey
Bronnikov, Sergei
coredata.dc:description: Hydrophobic N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were prepared using citric acid and L-phenylalanine through hydrothermal synthesis at optimum conditions (temperature T = 200 °C and time t = 9 h) and thoroughly investigated with different techniques. In organic solvents (ethanol, chloroform, dimethyl formamide, and xylene) under 350 nm excitation wavelength, N-CQDs exhibit intensive blue photoluminescence (PL) with the quantum yield equal to 36.5 %. Being embedded into polymer matrices (poly(methyl methacrylate) and epoxy resin), N-CQDs preserved their high PL.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots
Organic precursors
Hydrothermal synthesis
Photoluminescent composite films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are thankful to Dr. Olga Zhigalina, Shubnikov Institute of Crystallography, Moscow, Russian Federation, for providing TEM images of N-CQDs. The vibrational and XPS spectra measurement were performed at the Resource Centers (Center for optical and laser materials research and Resource Center Physical methods of surface investigation) of Research Park of Saint Petersburg State University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Exemplary PL spectra of N-CQDs samples obtained at 200 °C for different synthesis time, (a) 2 h and (b) 9 h, depending on excitation wavelength; (c) PL peak position of the N-CQDs samples obtained for different synthesis time depending on excitation wavelength, and (d) PL QY of the N-CQDs samples depending on their synthesis time.
(a) TEM image of N-CQDs and (b) their statistical diameter distribution.
XPS spectra of N-CQDs in (a) total energy range, (b) C1s range, (c) N1s range, and (d) O1s range.
Raman spectrum of N-CQDs (black line) deconvoluted for separation D (1368 cm−1) and G (1598 cm−1) bands in the 1125–1800 cm−1 region. Blue line corresponds to cumulative peak fitting.
Digital images of (a) PMMA/N-CQDs and (b) EP/N-CQDs composite films under UV irradiation.
Comparative data on conditions of the N-CQDs synthesis and N-CQDs characteristics.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots obtained from citric acid and L-phenylalanine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Russian Academy of Sciences, Institute of Macromolecular Compounds, St. Petersburg 199004, Russian Federation
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Russian Academy of Sciences, Institute of Macromolecular Compounds, St. Petersburg 199004, Russian Federation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Optical and Laser Materials Research, Research Park, St. Petersburg State University, St. Petersburg 198504, Russian Federation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Optical and Laser Materials Research
Research Park, St. Petersburg State University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Optical and Laser Materials Research, Research Park, St. Petersburg State University, St. Petersburg 198504, Russian Federation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, St. Petersburg State University, St. Petersburg 198504, Russian Federation
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, St. Petersburg State University, St. Petersburg 198504, Russian Federation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrophobic N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were prepared using citric acid and L-phenylalanine through hydrothermal synthesis at optimum conditions (temperature T = 200 °C and time t = 9 h) and thoroughly investigated with different techniques. In organic solvents (ethanol, chloroform, dimethyl formamide, and xylene) under 350 nm excitation wavelength, N-CQDs exhibit intensive blue photoluminescence (PL) with the quantum yield equal to 36.5 %. Being embedded into polymer matrices (poly(methyl methacrylate) and epoxy resin), N-CQDs preserved their high PL.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) attract significant interest due to their fascinating properties, such as intense tunable photoluminescence (PL), environmental friendliness, low cost, ease of fabrication, chemical and photo stability, biocompatibility, and nontoxicity. These properties provide CQDs application in bioimaging, bio- and chemo-sensing, photocatalysis, and as component materials for optoelectronic and electroluminescent devices [1–4].

There are two basic strategies, top-down and bottom-up, to obtain CQDs. The top-down strategy implies cutting the volumetric carbon particles resulting in formation of defective multi-layer graphene structures. Techniques employed in this approach are chemical oxidation, laser ablation, arc discharge, and electrochemical methods. Traditional carbon materials (such as graphite, graphene oxide, carbon fibers, carbon nanotubes, and carbon black [1–4]) and coal-derived resources (such as bituminous coal, anthracite, lignite, coal tar, and coke [5]) were used as carbon precursors in this strategy. The bottom-up approach has gained particular attention because of ability of CQDs production from natural organic resources using principles of green chemistry [4,6–8]. The procedures related to the bottom-up strategy include pyrolysis, hydro/solvatothermal synthesis, and microwave treatment of organic precursors [1–4]. Among organic precursors, citric acid (CA) seems to be one of the most representative source material for CQDs production [9–11]. However, CA-derived CQDs exhibit a relative low PL quantum yield (QY) and weak chemical and photo stability [9,12–14]. To increase CQDs QY, non-metal heteroatoms have suggested to be used as doping agents [15–18]. These heteroatoms are rich in electrons, which transfer to the surface of CQDs upon doping and shift their electron density, resulting in an increase in PL intensity [19]. The nitrogen atom is an attractive candidate for CQDs doping since its size is similar to that of carbon atom, and because of its electron donor character [11,18,20,21]. Indeed, a drastic improvement in CQDs QY was reported when nitrogen-containing agents were used for N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) production [14,18,22–25]. Natural amino acids are the most promising nitrogen sources. The structure of amino acids was found to have a significant impact on N-CQDs optical properties (such as emission wavelength, excitation wavelength-dependent PL, and QY) and, therefore, it determines the N-CQDs hydrophilic/hydrophobic behavior [22,26,27]. Among amino acids, L-phenyl alanine (Phe), as a dopant, was reported to provide higher N-CQDs QY [22,26]. The most researchers reported on hydrothermal N-CQDs synthesis when they used CA and Phe as the precursors [22,27–29]; we detected the only report on solvatothermal N-CQDs synthesis [26] and another one communication concerning microwave N-CQDs production [30]. The data related to conditions of the N-CQDs synthesis from CA and Phe and the resulted N-CQDs properties available from literature are summarized in Table 1 . For comparison, the data obtained in this work are also presented.

Table 1 demonstrates dispersion in synthesis conditions and PL properties of N-CQDs. Particularly, the synthesis temperature varies from 180 to 200 °C, whereas the synthesis duration differs from 8 to 30 h. Also, PL QY ranges from 5 to 90 %, the N-CQDs diameter spreads from 2 to 12.8 nm, and their λ em varies from 411 to 456 nm. Moreover, some authors [22,26,27] reported on hydrophobic character of the N-CQDs obtained from CA and Phe, whereas the other researchers [28,29] believed in their hydrophilic behavior. This aspect will be discussed later. A scattering in the presented data excited us to replicate the N-CQDs formation using CA and Phe as precursors and to search the optimum synthesis conditions for producing N-CQDs with high PL QY.

For the N-CQDs fabrication, we used CA monohydrate (Sigma-Aldrich, 99 % purity) and Phe (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥ 98 % purity) as precursors. They were further used as received. As a medium of hydrothermal synthesis, deionized water was used.

For N-CQDs/polymer composites preparation, we used the following polymer matrices: poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA, Sigma Aldrich, Mw = 15,000) with chloroform as a solvent and epoxy resin (EP) ED-20 (Chimex Ltd., Mw = 600) with polyethylene polyamine as a hardener.

Following recommendations available in literature [22,26], we prepared a mixture of 2.1 g (10 mmol) of CA and 1.65 g (10 mmol) of Phe. The mixture was dissolved in deionized water inside a 25 mL Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor, which was then encased into a muffle furnace. To find an optimum synthesis temperature, the precursors’ mixture in a muffle furnace was heated to either 180 or 200 °C. To evaluate optimum synthesis duration, the heating time ranged from 2 to 9 h at 1 h intervals. After reaction completing, the reactor was naturally cooled to room temperature.

The resulted product was then centrifuged at 4000 r.p.m. The precipitate obtained after centrifugation was then dissolved in ethanol and the solution was filtered through 0.22 µm PVDF syringe filter. The N-CQDs nanoparticles were precipitated from the alcohol solution in excess of distilled water. Then the alcohol-soluble part was separated in a centrifuge and dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 24 h. The dried solid product was placed in an exsiccator for storage. Dried N-CQDs samples and their solutions were further characterized with different methods.

We wish to emphasize a hydrophobic character of obtained N-CQDs. The N-CQDs structure is generally accepted to be comprised by two basic elements: a dense carbon core and a loose shell [22,26]. Following Pandit et al. [26], we can propose two-steps hydrothermal CQDs synthesis using CA and amino acid, Phe, as precursors. During the first step, a CA carbonization process leading to formation of the core occurs. During the second step, the amino acid molecules are efficiently conjugated to the core surface and form the shell. Since Phe relates to hydrophobic amino acid [22], the N-CQD shell is also hydrophobic. Therefore, obtained N-CQDs cannot be dissolved in water. However, they were detected to be solved in ethanol, chloroform, dimethyl formamide, and xylene. We should remind that the authors reported on hydrophilic N-CQDs obtained from CA and Phe precursors [28,29], sampled and liophilized a water-soluble fraction of the solvatothermal reaction product. Meanwhile, other researchers ([22,26,27] and present work) believed that a precipitate obtained after the reaction product centrifugation consisted of the N-CQDs, which could be dissolved in either ethanol or other organic solvent.

The PL characteristics of N-CQDs were investigated using a RF-6000 spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu). The samples were scanned in the wavelength range of 300–650 nm at the excitation wavelengths λ ex varying from 320 to 390 nm. To determine PL QY, we used quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a standard solution at the 350 nm excitation wavelength. Details of the QY evaluation are available in Supplementary data. The morphological properties of CQDs were analyzed with high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) technique using a Tecnai Osiris device (Thermo Fisher Scientific) operating at a 200 kV accelerating voltage. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectra were recorded with a SF-2000 UV-spectrophotometer (LOMO) in the wavelength range of 200–550 nm. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were realized using an Escalab 250Xi integrated photoelectron and scanning Auger electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The spectra were performed using a hemispherical energy analyzer with double focusing in the energy range from 0 to 1400 eV at a pressure of 10−10 mbar at room temperature. The Al-Kα line (energy 1486.6 eV) was used as an X-ray source. For XPS sample preparation, a portion of CQDs was dissolved in chloroform, poured onto silicon substrate, and dried. The resulting XPS bands were approximated by Gaussian peaks. The FTIR spectrum was obtained with a Nicolet 8700 (Thermo Scientific) spectrometer equipped with an attenuated total reflection (ATR) accessory with diamond as the ATR crystal. The spectrum was obtained in the 650–4000 cm−1 region. The spectral resolution was 4 cm−1. The number of scans was 150 items. The detector was MCT-A with the liquid nitrogen cooling. The Blackman–Harris window function was used as an apodization function. The Mertz method was used for phase correction. The Raman spectrum was obtained at a Senterra (Bruker) Raman spectrometer in the backscattering geometry. The excitation of Raman scattering was performed with the solid state 785 nm laser. The aperture was 25 × 1000 μm. The diffraction grating was 400 l/mm. The laser power under × 20 objective (numerical aperture 0.4) was about 1 mW.

Fig. 1a,b depict exemplarily the PL spectra of the N-CQDs samples obtained at 200 °C for selected synthesis time. Fig. 1 c demonstrates a change in the wavelength PL peak position as a function of excitation wavelength λex. Fig. 1b,c show that at t = 2 h the PL maximum strongly shifts towards longer wavelength with increasing excitation wavelength. This finding implies multiplicity of emission centers arising during the N-CQDs synthesis. Meanwhile, for longer synthesis duration, a weak dependence of the PL peak position versus excitation wavelength can be recognized. This means homogeneity of the N-CQDs structure [9,32].

To choose the optimal hydrothermal heating duration, we evaluated PL QY of the N-CQDs samples obtained for different synthesis time. Details of QY evaluating procedure are available in Fig. S1 and Table S1. As follows from Fig. 1d, QY increases with increasing synthesis time and achieves the maximum value of 36.5 % at t = 9 h.

As follows from Fig. 1b, the most intensive PL emission occurs at λ ex = 350 nm. An intensive blue PL emission of the N-CQDs sample, obtained at T = 200 °C for t = 9 h and dissolved in ethanol could be recognized in the insert of Fig. 1b. A strong PL emission was also observed when N-CQDs were dissolved in chloroform, dimethyl formamide, and xylene. We detected no evident solvatation effect [33], when compared PL spectra (λ ex = 350 nm) of N-CQDs dissolved in these solvents.

Also, we investigated PL intensity of N-CQDs obtained at 180 °C as a function of the synthesis time (Fig. S2). When we evaluated PL QY as a function of the synthesis time (Fig. S3), we concluded that for the maximum synthesis time (t = 8 h), QY = 7.4 % only (Table S2). Taking this finding into account, we drew a conclusion: T = 200 °C and t = 9 h could be treated as the optimum condition of the N-CQDs hydrothermal synthesis using CA and Phe as precursors. Hereafter, we will present the results of our experimental investigations of the N-CQDs obtained at the above mentioned optimal conditions exceptionally.

The TEM image of N-CQDs is presented in Fig. 2 a. In the image, N-CQDs look like nanoparticles of a nearly spherical shape. Their statistical diameter distribution is depicted in Fig. 2b. The N-CQDs diameter scatters between 1.25 and 3.5 nm around the mean diameter value of 2.4 nm.

Fig. 3 shows the UV–vis spectrum of N-CQDs, in which two absorption bands, at 260 nm and 340 nm, can be recognized. The peak at 260 nm is attributed to the π-π* electron transitions of the C = C bonds [22,26,28,30,32,34,35] in sp2-hybridized carbon core of N-CQDs. Another peak at 340 nm is related to the n-π* electron transition of the C = O bonds in the functional groups attached to the carbon core [22,27,30,32,34,35].

In Fig. 4 , the FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs is presented. The spectrum can be divided into two regions. In the high frequency region, multiple bands related to stretching C-H bonds can be found. The most notable of them are the sharp bands with maxima at 2925 and 2853 cm−1. They were assigned to asymmetric and symmetric C–H stretching vibration in –CH2– aliphatic fragments, respectively [29]. The less intensive peaks in the 3000–3100 cm−1 region ascribed to the hydrogen stretching vibrations in = C-H bonds, mostly originating from aromatic fragments [22,28–30,36], can be recognized. The broad shoulders in the 3100–3650 cm−1 region could be attributed to the vibrations of hydrogen bonded hydroxyl and amino groups. A weak band related to N-H stretching vibrations at 3457 cm−1 is also observed. A broad band with a maximum at 2547 cm−1 can be found. It relates to strong intramolecular hydrogen bonds [34,36,37]. The similar peaks were also detected in COOH functionalized CQDs [34].

In the region below 1850 cm−1, the most intensive band at 1700 cm−1 relates to the C = O stretching vibrations. This band overlaps the weaker bands at 1776 and 1740 cm−1, which are manifested in the spectrum as shoulders [34,35]. The peak at 1600 cm−1 is ascribed to the C = C stretching vibrations in sp2-hybridized carbon domains [27,34,36]. The aromatic C–H out-of-plane bending vibrations are observed at 745 and 700 cm−1. Two bands at 1495 and 1450 cm−1 are attributed to predominantly –CH2– scissoring deformations [38]. A broad band at 1160 cm−1 corresponds to C-O bonds stretching vibrations, probably originating from ethers and/or esters [36]. This band overlaps the 1266 cm−1 band related to the C-N stretching vibrations and C-O stretching vibrations in carboxylic groups [29,30,36]. The band at about 1100 cm−1 relates to C-OH stretching vibrations [36,39]. Moreover, the bands in the 1350–1390 cm−1 region, could probably be related to the COH bending vibrations [36].

Since the characteristic –OH bands as well as N-H stretching bands in the 3500–3000 cm−1 region exhibit relative low absorbance as compared with that related to hydroxyl and amino groups in functionalized CQDs [34], we concluded that the content of both hydroxyl and amino groups in the N-CQDs structure is quite low. Moreover, these groups can contribute to formation the strong hydrogen intramolecular interactions in the obtained N-CQDs. The low content of hydrophilic groups was experimentally proved by hydrophobicity of the synthesized N-CQDs.

The surface composition and chemical environments were detected with a XPS technique. In the XPS survey spectrum of N-CQDs shown in Fig. 5 a, the peaks related to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s elements could be recognized at binding energies at 285, 398, and 532 eV, respectively. When considering only C, O, and N atoms in N-CQDs, their percentage in N-CQDs composition is as follows: C = 85.5 %, O = 11.0 %, and N = 3.5 %. We should emphasize a high carbon content in the N-CQDs surface structure; it exceeds those for N-CQDs available in literature [18,28,29,31] and confirms formation of carbonized structure in the obtained N-CQDs.

The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum presented in Fig. 5b was deconvoluted using three peaks related to C-C (284.3 eV), C-N/C-O (285.6 eV), and O-C = O (288.3 eV) bonds. The deconvoluted high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 5c) demonstrates two peaks attributed to N-H (400.0 eV) and C-N (402.1 eV) bonds. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 5d) was deconvoluted using three peaks representing H-O-C = O (530.9 eV), C = O (531.6 eV), and C-O-H (532.8 eV) bonds. Therefore, the surface components of N-CQDs determined from the XPS spectra are in a good agreement with the results obtained from FTIR examination.

Fig. 6 demonstrates a fragment of the Raman spectrum of N-CQDs in the 1100–1800 cm−1 range, where two intensive overlapped bands could clearly be recognized. After spectrum deconvolution with two Voigt contours, their positions become distinct. The maximum of the D band related to sp3-hybridized defects in the N-CQDs structure is located at 1368 cm−1, whereas a peak of the G band referred to sp2-hybridized structure and representing the amount of graphitization associated with CQDs [32,40,41] is placed at 1598 cm−1. The intensity ratio of the G and D peaks was found to be IG/ID = 1.17. This value indicates certain predomination of graphitic crystalline structures in obtained N-CQDs [32,42].

We prepared PMMA/N-CQDs composite films by drop casting the polymer solution with N-CQDs onto a Teflon substrate and consequent drying at standard conditions. For EP/N-CQDs film preparation, we preliminarily dissolved N-CQDs in a hardener and then mixed it with EP. We carried out the curing process at room temperature in a Petri dish treated with a release agent. The content of N-CQDs in both polymer matrices was about 1 wt%.

In Fig. 7 , the digital images of the prepared composite films under UV irradiation (λ ex = 365 nm) are presented. They demonstrate intensive blue emission of the films. This ensures N-CQDs perfect distribution and preserving their PL with a similar λ em in the polymer matrices. The prepared composite films were reported to be used for light-emitted diodes fabrication [43] and for improving the perovskite solar cells performance [44].

In conclusion, we reported on hydrothermal synthesis of photoluminescent hydrophobic N-CQDs using CA and Phe as precursors. To find optimum condition of their obtaining, we carried out our syntheses at two temperatures, 200 °C and 180 °C, for time varying from 2 to 9 h. We concluded that the N-CQDs samples synthesized at 200 °C for 9 h demonstrated the maximum PL QY = 36.5 % evaluated from their PL spectrum. These conditions were accepted as the optimum ones. From the TEM image, N-CQDs were detected to be nearly spherical particles with a mean diameter of 2.4 nm. When we analyzed the UV–vis, FTIR, and XPS spectra of N-CQDs, we revealed the presence of the functional groups on their surface. Meanwhile, we detected a lack of hydrophilic groups, ensuring hydrophobic character of synthesized N-CQDs. Using the Raman and UV–vis spectra, we ascertained the availability of sp3- and sp2-hybridized carbon core of N-CQDs. From the XPS-spectrum in the full energy range, the portion of carbon in the N-CQDs structure was found to be as high as 85.5 %. The polymer/N-CQDs nanocomposite films were prepared using PMMA and EP as matrices. The films exhibited an intensive blue PL under 350 nm excitation wavelength, ensuring a good dispersion of N-CQDs nanoparticles and their high photoluminescent ability in polymer matrices.

Sergei Kostromin: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Anastasia Borodina: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Dmitrii Pankin: Investigation, Formal analysis. Alexey Povolotskiy: Investigation, Formal analysis. Sergei Bronnikov: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Sergei Bronnikov reports financial support was provided by FBIS Institute of Macromolecular Compounds. Sergei Bronnikov reports a relationship with FBIS Institute of Macromolecular Compounds that includes: employment. Sergei Bronnikov has patent pending to copyright. The authors declare that there is no a conflict of interest If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful to Dr. Olga Zhigalina, Shubnikov Institute of Crystallography, Moscow, Russian Federation, for providing TEM images of N-CQDs. The vibrational and XPS spectra measurement were performed at the Resource Centers (Center for optical and laser materials research and Resource Center Physical methods of surface investigation) of Research Park of Saint Petersburg State University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2024.141175.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
